rely on the circulation of air directly over [heat dissipation fins](/wiki/Fin_(extended_surface) "Fin (extended surface)") or hot areas of the [engine](/wiki/Engine "Engine") to cool them in order to keep the engine within operating temperatures. Air-cooled designs are far simpler than their liquid-cooled counterparts, which require a separate [radiator](/wiki/Radiator_(engine_cooling) "Radiator (engine cooling)"), coolant reservoir, piping and pumps.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of engine

Main article: [Internal combustion engine cooling](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine_cooling "Internal combustion engine cooling")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Motorcycle_engine_19_2012.jpg/250px-Motorcycle_engine_19_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorcycle_engine_19_2012.jpg)

Air-cooled engines（[Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Motor_Company "Yamaha Motor Company") [XV1900A](/wiki/Yamaha_XV1900A "Yamaha XV1900A")）

**Air-cooled engines** rely on the circulation of air directly over [heat dissipation fins](/wiki/Fin_(extended_surface) "Fin (extended surface)") or hot areas of the [engine](/wiki/Engine "Engine") to cool them in order to keep the engine within operating temperatures. Air-cooled designs are far simpler than their liquid-cooled counterparts, which require a separate [radiator](/wiki/Radiator_(engine_cooling) "Radiator (engine cooling)"), coolant reservoir, piping and pumps.

Air-cooled engines are widely seen in applications where weight or simplicity is the primary goal. Their simplicity makes them suited for uses in small applications like [chainsaws](/wiki/Chainsaw "Chainsaw") and [lawn mowers](/wiki/Lawn_mower "Lawn mower"), as well as small [generators](/wiki/Engine-generator "Engine-generator") and similar roles. These qualities also make them highly suitable for aviation use, where they are widely used in [general aviation aircraft](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") and as [auxiliary power units](/wiki/Auxiliary_power_unit "Auxiliary power unit") on larger aircraft. Their simplicity, in particular, also makes them common on [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Newjug1.jpg/250px-Newjug1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newjug1.jpg)

A cylinder from an air-cooled aviation engine, a [Continental C85](/wiki/Continental_C85 "Continental C85"). Notice the rows of fins on both the [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") cylinder barrel and the [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") cylinder head. The fins provide additional surface area for air to pass over the cylinder and absorb heat.

## Introduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction")]

Most modern [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine") are cooled by a closed circuit carrying liquid [coolant](/wiki/Antifreeze_(coolant) "Antifreeze (coolant)") through channels in the engine block and cylinder head. A fluid in these channels absorbs heat and then flows to a [heat exchanger](/wiki/Heat_exchanger "Heat exchanger") or [radiator](/wiki/Radiator "Radiator") where the coolant releases heat into the air (or [raw water](/wiki/Raw_water "Raw water"), in the case of [marine engines](/wiki/Marine_engine "Marine engine")). Thus, while they are not *ultimately* cooled by the liquid, as the heat is exchanged with some other fluid like air, because of the liquid-coolant circuit they are known as *liquid-cooled*.

In contrast, heat generated by an air-cooled engine is released directly into the air. Typically this is facilitated with metal [fins](/wiki/Fin_(extended_surface) "Fin (extended surface)") covering the outside of the cylinder head and [cylinders](/wiki/Cylinder_(engine) "Cylinder (engine)") which increase the surface area that air can act on. Air may be force fed with the use of a fan and shroud to achieve efficient cooling with high volumes of air or simply by natural air flow with well designed and angled fins.

In all combustion engines, a great percentage of the heat generated, around 44%, escapes through the exhaust. Another 8% or so ends up in the [oil](/wiki/Motor_oil "Motor oil"), which itself has to be cooled in an [oil cooler](/wiki/Oil_cooler "Oil cooler"). This means less than half of the heat has to be removed through other systems. In an air-cooled engine, only about 12% of the heat flows out through the metal fins.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Air cooled engines usually run noisier, however it provides more simplicity which gives benefits when it comes to servicing and part replacement and is usually cheaper to be maintained.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Road vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Road vehicles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/%E4%BB%8A%E5%B8%82%E5%B8%82%E5%B0%8F%E5%80%89%E3%81%AB%E3%81%A6.jpg/250px-%E4%BB%8A%E5%B8%82%E5%B8%82%E5%B0%8F%E5%80%89%E3%81%AB%E3%81%A6.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%BB%8A%E5%B8%82%E5%B8%82%E5%B0%8F%E5%80%89%E3%81%AB%E3%81%A6.jpg)

Honda CB1100

Many [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle") use air cooling for the sake of reducing weight and complexity. Few current production automobiles have air-cooled engines (such as [Tatra 815](/wiki/Tatra_815 "Tatra 815")), but historically it was common for many high-volume vehicles. The orientation of the engine cylinders is commonly found in either single-cylinder or coupled in groups of two, and cylinders are commonly oriented in a horizontal fashion as a [flat engine](/wiki/Flat_engine "Flat engine"), while vertical [straight-four engine](/wiki/Straight-four_engine "Straight-four engine") have been used. Examples of past air-cooled road vehicles, in roughly chronological order, include:

* [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin_(automobile) "Franklin (automobile)") (1902-1934)
* [New Way](/w/index.php?title=New_Way_(automobile)&action=edit&redlink=1 "New Way (automobile) (page does not exist)") (1905) - limited production run out from the "CLARKMOBILE"
* [Chevrolet Series M Copper-Cooled](/wiki/Chevrolet_Series_M_Copper-Cooled "Chevrolet Series M Copper-Cooled") (1921-1923) (very few built)[[3]](#cite_note-Sloan1964pp71-94-3)
* [Tatra](/wiki/Tatra_(company) "Tatra (company)") all-wheel-drive military trucks
* [Tatra 11](/wiki/Tatra_11 "Tatra 11") (1923-1927) and subsequent models
* [Tatra T77](/wiki/Tatra_T77 "Tatra T77") (1934-1938)
* [Tatra T87](/wiki/Tatra_T87 "Tatra T87") (1936-1950)
* [Tatra T97](/wiki/Tatra_T97 "Tatra T97") (1936-1939)
* [Tatra T600 Tatraplan](/wiki/Tatra_T600_Tatraplan "Tatra T600 Tatraplan") (1946-1952)
* [Tatra T603](/wiki/Tatra_T603 "Tatra T603") (1955-1975)
* [Tatra T613](/wiki/Tatra_T613 "Tatra T613") (1974-1996)
* [Tatra T700](/wiki/Tatra_T700 "Tatra T700") (1996-1999)
* [Crosley](/wiki/Crosley_Motors "Crosley Motors") (1939-1945)
* The East German [Trabant](/wiki/Trabant "Trabant") (1957-1991)
* [Trabant 500](/wiki/Trabant_500 "Trabant 500") (1957-1962)
* [Trabant 600](/wiki/Trabant_600 "Trabant 600") (1962-1965)
* [Trabant 601](/wiki/Trabant_601 "Trabant 601") (1964-1989)
* [ZAZ Zaporozhets](/wiki/Zaporozhets "Zaporozhets") (1958-1994)
* [Fiat 500](/wiki/Fiat_500 "Fiat 500") (1957-1975)
* [Fiat 126](/wiki/Fiat_126 "Fiat 126") (1972-1987)
* [Porsche 356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356") (1948-1965)
* [Porsche 911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911") (1964-1998)
* [Porsche 912](/wiki/Porsche_912 "Porsche 912") (1965-1969, 1976)
* [VW-Porsche 914](/wiki/Porsche_914 "Porsche 914") (1969-1976)
* The [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle"), [Type 2](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_2 "Volkswagen Type 2"), [SP2](/wiki/Volkswagen_SP2 "Volkswagen SP2"), [Karmann Ghia](/wiki/Volkswagen_Karmann_Ghia "Volkswagen Karmann Ghia"), and [Type 3](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_3 "Volkswagen Type 3") all utilized the [same air-cooled engine](/wiki/Volkswagen_air-cooled_engine "Volkswagen air-cooled engine") (1938-2013) with various displacements
* [Volkswagen Type 2 (T3)](/wiki/Vanagon#Petrol/gasoline "Vanagon") (1979–1982)
* [Volkswagen Type 4](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_4 "Volkswagen Type 4") (1968-1974)
* [Volkswagen Gol (G1)](/wiki/Volkswagen_Gol#First_Generation_(1980-1994) "Volkswagen Gol") (1980-1986)
* [Toyota U engine](/wiki/Toyota_U_engine "Toyota U engine") (1961-1976)
* [Chevrolet Corvair](/wiki/Chevrolet_Corvair "Chevrolet Corvair") (1960-1969)
* [Citroën 2CV](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_2CV "Citroën 2CV") (1948-1990) (Featured a high pressure oil cooling system, and used a fan bolted to the crankshaft end)
* [Citroën GS](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_GS "Citroën GS") and [GSA](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_GSA "Citroën GSA")
* [Honda 1300](/wiki/Honda_1300 "Honda 1300") (1969-1973)
* [NSU Prinz](/wiki/NSU_Prinz "NSU Prinz")
* [Royal Enfield Motorcycles (India)](/wiki/Royal_Enfield_(India) "Royal Enfield (India)"): The 350cc and 500cc Twinspark motorcycle engines are air-cooled
* [Oltcit Club](/wiki/Oltcit_Club "Oltcit Club") (1981–1995)T13/653, G11/631 and VO36/630
* [Demak Dzm 200 2015](/w/index.php?title=Demak_Dzm_200_2015&action=edit&redlink=1 "Demak Dzm 200 2015 (page does not exist)")

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aviation")]

During the 1920s and 30s there was a great debate in the aviation industry about the merits of air-cooled vs. liquid-cooled designs. At the beginning of this period, the liquid used for cooling was water at ambient pressure. The amount of heat carried away by a fluid is a function of its capacity and the difference in input and output temperatures. As the [boiling point](/wiki/Boiling_point "Boiling point") of water is reduced with lower pressure, and the water could not be efficiently pumped as steam, radiators had to have enough cooling power to account for the loss in cooling power as the aircraft climbed. The resulting radiators were quite large and caused a significant amount of [aerodynamic drag](/wiki/Aerodynamic_drag "Aerodynamic drag").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197153-4)

This placed the two designs roughly equal in terms of power to drag, but the air-cooled designs were almost always lighter and simpler. In 1921, the [US Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy"), largely due to the efforts of Commander [Bruce G. Leighton](/w/index.php?title=Bruce_G._Leighton&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bruce G. Leighton (page does not exist)"), decided that the simplicity of the air-cooled design would result in less maintenance workload, which was paramount given the limited working area of [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier"). Leighton's efforts led to the Navy underwriting air-cooled engine development at [Pratt & Whitney](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney "Pratt & Whitney") and [Wright Aeronautical](/wiki/Wright_Aeronautical "Wright Aeronautical").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197153-4)

Most other groups, especially in Europe where aircraft performance was rapidly improving, were more concerned with the issue of drag. While air-cooled designs were common on light aircraft and trainers, as well as some [transport aircraft](/wiki/Military_transport_aircraft "Military transport aircraft") and [bombers](/wiki/Bomber "Bomber"), liquid-cooled designs remained much more common for [fighters](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft") and high-performance bombers. The drag issue was upset by the 1929 introduction of the [NACA cowl](/wiki/NACA_cowl "NACA cowl"), which greatly reduced the drag of air-cooled engines in spite of their larger frontal area, and the drag related to cooling was at this point largely even.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197153-4)

In the late 1920s into the 1930s, a number of European companies introduced cooling systems that kept the water under pressure allowed it to reach much higher temperatures without boiling, carrying away more heat and thus reducing the volume of water required and the size of the radiator by as much as 30%, which opened the way to a new generation of high-powered, relatively low-drag liquid cooled inline engines such as the [Rolls-Royce Merlin](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Merlin "Rolls-Royce Merlin") and [Daimler-Benz DB601](/wiki/Daimler-Benz_DB_601 "Daimler-Benz DB 601"), which had an advantage over the unpressurised early versions of the [Jumo 211](/wiki/Junkers_Jumo_211 "Junkers Jumo 211"). This also led to development work attempting to eliminate the radiator entirely using [evaporative cooling](/wiki/Evaporative_cooling "Evaporative cooling"), allowing it to turn to steam and running the steam through tubes located just under the skin of the wings and fuselage, where the fast moving outside air condensed it back to water. While this concept was used on a number of record-setting aircraft in the late 1930s, it always proved impractical for production aircraft for a wide variety of reasons.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197155-5)

In 1929, [Curtiss](/wiki/Curtiss_Aeroplane_and_Motor_Company "Curtiss Aeroplane and Motor Company") began experiments replacing water with [ethylene glycol](/wiki/Ethylene_glycol "Ethylene glycol") in a [Curtiss D-12](/wiki/Curtiss_D-12 "Curtiss D-12") engine. Ethylene glycol could run up to 250°C and reduced the radiator size by 50% compared to water-cooled designs. The experiments were extremely successful and by 1932 the company had switched all future designs to this coolant. At the time, [Union Carbide](/wiki/Union_Carbide "Union Carbide") held a monopoly on the industrial process to make ethylene glycol, so it was initially used only in the US, with [Allison Engines](/wiki/Allison_Engine_Company "Allison Engine Company") picking it up soon after. It was not until the mid-1930s that [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce "Rolls-Royce") adopted it as supplies improved, converting all of their engines to ethylene glycol. With the much smaller radiators and less fluid in the system, the weight and drag of these designs was well below contemporary air-cooled designs. On a weight basis, these liquid-cooled designs offered as much as 30% better performance.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197156-6)

In the late- and post-war era, the high-performance field quickly moved to [jet engines](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine"). This took away the primary market for late-model liquid-cooled engines. Those roles that remained with piston power were mostly slower designs and civilian aircraft. In these roles, the simplicity and reduction in servicing needs is far more important than drag, and from the end of the war on almost all piston aviation engines have been air-cooled, with few exceptions.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor197156-6)

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit), most of the engines manufactured by [Lycoming](/wiki/Lycoming_Engines "Lycoming Engines") and [Continental](/wiki/Teledyne_Continental_Motors "Teledyne Continental Motors") are used by major manufacturers of light aircraft [Cirrus](/wiki/Cirrus_Design "Cirrus Design"), [Cessna](/wiki/Cessna "Cessna") and so on. Other engine manufactures using air-cooled engine technology are [ULPower](/wiki/ULPower "ULPower") and [Jabiru](/wiki/Jabiru_Aircraft "Jabiru Aircraft"), more active in the Light-Sport Aircraft ([LSA](/wiki/Light-sport_aircraft "Light-sport aircraft")) and [ultralight aircraft](/wiki/Ultralight_aircraft "Ultralight aircraft") market. [Rotax](/wiki/Rotax "Rotax") uses a combination of air-cooled cylinders and liquid-cooled cylinder heads.

### Diesel engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Diesel engines")]

Some small diesel engines, e.g. those made by [Deutz AG](/wiki/Deutz_AG "Deutz AG") and [Lister Petter](/wiki/Lister_Petter "Lister Petter") are air-cooled. Probably the only big Euro 5 truck air-cooled engine (V8 320 kW power 2100 N·m torque one) is being produced by [Tatra](/wiki/Tatra_(company) "Tatra (company)"). BOMAG part of the FAYAT group also utilizes an air cooled inline 6 cylinder motor, in many of their construction vehicles.

### Stationary or portable engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Air-cooled_engine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Stationary or portable engines")]

Stationary or portable engines were commercially introduced early in the 1900s. The first commercial production was by the New Way Motor Company of [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan"), Michigan, US. The company produced air-cooled engines in single and twin cylinders in both horizontal and vertical cylinder format. Subsequent to their initial production which was exported worldwide, other companies took up the advantages of this cooling method, especially in small portable engines. Applications include mowers, generators, outboard motors, pump sets, saw benches and auxiliary power plants and more.
